content:
This is the last structure of the teaching for the ICT mentorship, focusing on ICT short-term top-down
analysis, specifically from four hours down to five minutes.
This is a personal approach to how all previously taught concepts are employed from a four-hour down to a
five-minute time frame.

The focus of this presentation is to:
- Determine the impact of the four-hour perspective on a given asset.
- Identify directional bias with a high time frame intraday four-hour chart.
- Classify the PD Arrays accurately to assist in key levels.
- Complete institutional analysis on a four-hour basis.

- DAY OF THE WEEK & HIGHER TIME FRAME CONTEXT
The beginning of the analysis starts on a four-hour chart, working down into lower time frames. The first
thing in mind is the day of the week we're trading.
Preferably, this involves a weekend analysis, watching for Sunday's open, any gaps, and early week
momentum. Typically, I observe Monday to anticipate Tuesday and Wednesday's potential moves.
All monthly, weekly, and daily analysis is included when viewing the four-hour perspective.

- TRUE DAY
After determining the day of the week and potential profiles/templates, I look at Intraday Price Action (IPTA)
and define it in 'True Day.'
I ensure I'm only focusing on setups within the parameters that make up 'True Day,' as taught in the
mentorship.

- KILL ZONES
Next, I look at specific times of the day, which are the Kill Zones:
- London Open
- New York Open
- London Close
- Asia

I include the Central Bank Dealer's Range, incorporating that range in price, looking for consolidations
within it.
I look for standard deviations to align with potential highs and lows of the day.

The 'Flout' combines the Asian Range and Central Bank Dealer's Range time-limited to create a total
range high and low.
I look for standard deviations for that combined range. More in-depth discussion will follow.

What do the intraday models suggest about price action?
- Will there be a down day with an upward swing first in London?
- Or a quiet London session followed by a run in New York?
I refer to potential intraday profiles.

section_title: INTEGRATING HIGHER TIME FRAME ANALYSIS TO INTRADAY
content:
All these insights, in addition to analysis ideas transposed from the monthly, weekly, and daily charts, are
taken from the four-hour chart and carried over into the intraday charts.

Here, we decide which lower time frame to drop down to from the four-hour chart:
- 60 Minute chart
- 30 Minute chart
- 15 Minute chart
- Five Minute chart
I don't include a one-minute chart because I don't like them. You need to decide where you're going to go
from the four-hour. I don't want to push you into a specific mode because there are many ways to look at
time.

60 Minutes is a decent time frame, but it still needs more refinement. Personally, I like to go from a four
- hour down to a 30-minute or 15-minute chart.
Then, I further refine it for a five-minute entry if possible. While the best is to get entries on a five-minute
chart, I don't always have that luxury. My setups usually confirm entries on a 15-minute minimum.

section_title: FLEXIBILITY IN TIME FRAME AND IDENTIFYING FAIR VALUE GAPS
content:
The choice of the next time frame down from four-hour is flexible to allow for a personalized approach.
Lower time frames below four-hour may be muddy on a 60-minute chart but clearer on a 15-minute or five
- minute chart.
When dropping down from a four-hour, the key is to look for a time frame that produces Fair Value Gaps.
You might not see a Fair Value Gap on a one-hour chart, but it may exist on a 15-minute time frame. This
allows you to pick your PD Arrays relative to gaps, bullish order blocks, or breakers. Higher time frames
smooth out price, while lower time frames create those pockets of illiquidity that need refinement.

section_title: DAY OF THE WEEK TRADING STRATEGY
content:
Using previous daily, weekly, and monthly analysis, I look for reasons to trade in that higher time frame
directional bias:
- If I'm bearish from the higher time frame, I look for shorts on Mondays, Tuesdays, and Wednesdays.
- If I'm bullish from the higher time frame, I look for longs on Mondays, Tuesdays, and Wednesdays.

If a Monday, Tuesday, or Wednesday influence (weekly or daily templates) does not materialize, I look for
late week scenarios on Thursday and Friday to come into fruition. This means adjusting expectations to
Thursday and Friday templates and aligning with potential weekly profiles.

- OPTIMAL TRADING HOURS AND OBJECTIVES
I look for setups from the higher time frame analysis within the hours defined by 'True Day.' The bulk of the
daily volume is typically between 3 AM and 10 AM New York time.
I want to either position myself correctly ahead of this window or during the first half of it. After the New
York Open, I lower my expectations and am content with smaller intraday objectives.

section_title: BULLISH KILL ZONE STRATEGY (LONDON OPEN, NEW YORK OPEN)
content:
Utilizing higher time frame analysis:
- I look for a trade setup in the London Open Kill Zone.
- I aim for the low of the day when the higher time frame is bullish.
- If I fail in London, I look for a New York Open setup to reposition or get a new position.

section_title: BEARISH KILL ZONE STRATEGY (LONDON OPEN, NEW YORK OPEN)
content:
Utilizing higher time frame analysis:
- I look for a trade setup in the London Open Kill Zone.
- I aim for the high of the day when the higher time frame is bearish.
- If I fail in London, I look for a New York Open setup to reposition or get a new position.

I look to collapse the bulk or all of my intraday positions starting at the 10 o'clock to 11 o'clock in the
morning New York time window. This is for profit taking or the beginnings of London Close.

When the higher time frame analysis suggests a bullish bias:
- I use one, two, or three negative standard deviations (going down) of the Central Bank Dealer's Range for
long entries.
- I look for 15
- to 60-minute discount arrays that overlap with these standard deviations to determine which
one to frame my entry on.

When the higher time frame analysis suggests a bearish bias:
- I use plus one, plus two, or plus three standard deviations of the Central Bank Dealer's Range for short
entries.
- I look for 15
- to 60-minute premium arrays to overlap with these standard deviations to determine which
one to frame my entry on.

section_title: BLENDING CBDR STANDARD DEVIATIONS WITH PD ARRAYS
content:
I'm looking for standard deviations (one, two, or three) higher when I want to go short, but I'm not simply
selling short based on deviations alone. I'm looking for a premium array, for example, a 60-minute time
frame, that will overlap with that same standard deviation.
It's a matter of blending these elements. I don't indiscriminately add deviations. I look for a 60-minute (less
than four-hour) or a 15-minute premium array. This could be a Fair Value Gap, or an old high that needs to
be run above for stops (a 'turtle soup'), but it must overlap with the Central Bank Dealer's Range deviation.
That's the key: blending those things together.
Consistency relies on using and understanding the PD Array Matrix.

- OPTIMAL SCENARIOS
If my higher time frame analysis is bullish:
- I will look to enter longs below the Asian Range High, preferably under the Asian Range Low (this is the
best scenario).
If my higher time frame analysis is bearish:
- I will look to enter shorts above the Asian Range Low, preferably above the Asian Range High (this is the
best scenario).

- HIGH PROBABILITY SCENARIOS
To rephrase for perfect understanding:
- If I'm bullish, the best scenario is to go long below the Asian Range Low. However, as long as I'm below
the Asian Range High, I'll still take what I consider high-probability longs.
- If I'm bearish, I preferably want to go short above the Asian Range High. But as long as I'm trading short
above the Asian Range Low, it's still defined as a high-probability scenario.

If I'm bullish, I expect the Asian Range High to be retested for entry as support or for adding to open long
positions. Many times, it will do that, then accelerate and expand towards the New York Open.
Sometimes, even during the New York Open, the Asian Range High, when bullish, will be retreated to and
retested as support, then rally again into London Close or even the next day.

section_title: ASIAN RANGE RETEST FOR SHORT ENTRIES
content:
The same applies in opposite terms for bearish scenarios: if I'm looking for an opportunity to go short and
the market has already moved down away from the Asian Range during New York, it can rally back up into
the Asian Range Low and retest that. This could be another opportunity to get short or add to an existing
position.

- AR & CBDR CONFLUENCE
For daily range projections, I look for standard deviations in the Asian Range to overlap with those seen in
the Central Bank Dealer's Range for the low and high of the day.
When Central Bank Dealer's Range and Asian Range confluences get really close to a level and it lines up
with a PD Array, chances are you're very close to the high or low of the day.

- DEFINITION AND STANDARD DEVIATION CALCULATION
The 'Flout' is the Central Bank Dealer's Range and the Asian Range combined. It's that whole time window
from the highest high to the lowest low, including both wicks and candle bodies.
If I am bullish, I look for overlapping in the total range of the Central Bank Dealer's Range and Asian Range
that has been divided in half. This half-range makes one standard deviation for the Flout. For example, if
the combined range from CBDR open to Asian Range close is 40 pips, half of that range (20 pips) is one
standard deviation for the Flout. I project this from the center point, going up one, two, three, or more
standard deviations. It's not the full range high to low.

Looking for confluences of Flout standard deviations, Central Bank Dealer's Range, and Asian Range with
discount arrays on the 60 to 15-minute chart is ideal for entries when bullish.
If I'm bearish, I look for overlapping in the total range of the Central Bank Dealer's Range and Asian Range
that has been divided in half as one standard deviation. Looking for confluences of Flout standard
deviations, Central Bank Dealer's Range, and Asian Range with premium arrays on a 60-minute to 15
- minute chart is ideal for entries.

The Flout is only half of the total range, and you start nesting them out lower and lower. The Flout can have
many standard deviations; there isn't a rule-based idea like there is for the Central Bank Dealer's Range or
Asian Range.
Asian Range can go up one or two standard deviations to create a high, or down one or two standard
deviations to create a low of the day. Flout can involve many standard deviations that have to keep being
applied and added to as the daily range expands. When we start approaching extremes in the day and high
premium levels on the 60-minute or 15-minute time frame, especially near London Close, you're probably
very close to the actual low or high of the day.

- LONDON SESSION
When I'm bearish, I look for the high of the day in London. When I'm bullish, I look for the low of the day in
London.

- NEW YORK OPEN CONTINUATION
When I'm bearish and the four-hour has not yet traded to a discount array, I expect the New York Open to
continue lower.
When I'm bullish and the four-hour has not yet traded to a premium array, I expect the New York Open to
continue higher.

- NEW YORK OPEN REVERSALS & AGREEMENT
How do I know there's going to be a New York session reversal?
- If price trades to a four-hour premium array, it's probably going to be a market reversal in New York, and it
will come lower.
- If price has been trading down and trades into a four-hour discount array during the New York session,
there's a high probability that will create a New York market reversal.
As long as these reversals have not happened or are yet to happen, chances are the New York session will
be a continuation of the higher time frame momentum and bias in institutional order flow. Until that
happens, London and New York will be in agreement in terms of direction. For more specific information,
refer to April's content on intraday profiles.

section_title: CALIBRATING KEY PRICE LEVELS WITH PD ARRAY MATRIX
content:
I note the key price levels once I determine a portion of market structure I want to use for my trade ideas.
This means defining a range, whether I'm operating as internal range liquidity or working off external range
liquidity.
I calibrate those levels from the PD Array Matrix to the nearest 10-level or five-level.

section_title: AVERAGE DAILY RANGE (ADR) FOR INTRADAY EXTREMES
content:
Finally, I look at the Average Daily Range projections. I use a five-day Average Daily Range to help me
determine possible intraday range extremes for the single day I'm trading.

section_title: BULLISH ADR PROJECTIONS AND FIBONACCI EXTENSIONS
content:
If I'm bullish, I look for the market to trade to the Average Daily Range High. If this Average Daily Range
High is broken, I use the Fibonacci tool on the Average Daily Range high and low.
I use the 127 extension and 162 extensions as targets. By themselves, they are nothing, but I look for a
premium array when the Average Daily Range High is broken. I look for the 127 extension to line up with a
premium array on a 60-minute or 15-minute time frame, or the 162 extension to overlap with a 60-minute or
15-minute premium array.

section_title: BEARISH ADR PROJECTIONS AND FIBONACCI EXTENSIONS
content:
The reverse is true when I'm bearish:
- I look for the market to trade to the Average Daily Range Low.
- If the Average Daily Range Low is broken and goes well beyond it, I use the Fibonacci on the Average
Daily Range high and low for a 127 or 162 extension for downside targets.
By themselves, these extensions don't mean anything. They must overlap with a discount array on a 15
- or
60-minute time frame.

section_title: BLENDING ALL PROJECTIONS FOR PRECISE TARGETS
content:
Blending these things together and incorporating Central Bank Dealer's Range standard deviations, Asian
Range standard deviations, and Flout standard deviations, you'll often get within 10 pips of the daily high
and low. That's why I aim to exit 10 pips before, as I might be wrong in my projections of where the high
and low may form.

section_title: PROFIT TAKING STRATEGY AND PROJECTION PHILOSOPHY
content:
Preferably, if I'm long and calculating the high, I want to be out definitively in the direction price is moving. I
won't hold for the last couple of pips. Every time I've tried, it's either frustrated me because it never reached
my target, or it fell short.
These are all projections, not absolutions. Standard deviations assist; they do not guarantee. Over time,
you'll see they have very good results in helping determine levels.

section_title: BLENDING MULTIPLE DEVIATIONS FOR HIGH ACCURACY
content:
There are multiple varying standard deviations from the Flout, Central Bank Dealer's Range, and Asian
Range. It's the blending of all three, plus the Average Daily Range.
If the Average Daily Range is broken, it signifies a big range day. To assess how big, you look back at the
standard deviations on CBDR, Flout, and Asian Range. You keep building them up on bullish days or
adding them down on bearish days, looking for the respective premium or discount array on the 15
- or 60
- minute time frame. When they overlap or converge, you're usually within 10 pips of the variance, which is
why I exit early.
I don't mind leaving the last 25-30 pips on the table, as long as I'm getting consistent range expansions and
a good piece of the move. I've never been perfect, and trying to be that accurate has always hurt me.
Taking profits when the train is moving in the right direction is always the right choice, whether on demo or
live.

- CONDITION, STAGE, EXECUTION
I've taught from the context of:
- Condition: Higher time frame directional bias.
- Stage: What sets your trade up, what it looks like.
- Execution: How to get in and out, whether using limit orders or market orders (this has been covered
elsewhere in the mentorship).
My main focus here is on identifying the setups, because once you understand where the market is going
and the setup, the entry aspect is easy.

section_title: THE IMPORTANCE OF SPECIALIZING IN ONE SETUP
content:
As a technical trader, you only need one setup or one pattern to trade on. Many tools have been taught,
and it can feel overwhelming. These lessons provide a step-by-step conceptual approach to how I use
them.
Each element I've mentioned has been explained in greater detail in previous lessons. This presentation
outlines the specific order in which I apply these concepts. Once you go through this list in the taught order,
you will arrive at the conditions for a trade setup.
I will teach you the two setups that I trade; I don't use anything else or look for any other patterns or
information. It's crucial to stick to one pattern, especially in the beginning, to measure consistency and
build confidence. It's normal for it not to work sometimes, or for you to do it wrong. Learn in a demo account
to give yourself permission to make mistakes without financial damage.

Over the next couple of slides, I will share the actual things I look for, how my mind interprets price, and my
go-to patterns. You'll find they are not overly complicated and will recognize them right away.
I don't use a slew of different things. While I can see ICT Stingers, reflection patterns, and other patterns
discussed in free tutorials and on YouTube, I let them pass. The patterns I execute on, whether in demo,
teaching, or live trades, are the ones I hunt for.

Please understand that I am not trying to force you into the mold of 'ICT patterns only.' You don't have to
use these exact patterns to be successful; there are other ways to use the information I've provided. Your
setups may be slightly different. These are the hallmarks of how I internalize and analyze the markets.
When I'm right, I attribute it to these patterns.
I'll share a few patterns to illustrate a simple yet effective approach, emphasizing searching for just one
setup for speculation in your demo account. Practice is key. These are by no means the only possible
patterns in the mentorship; I include my personal favorites and a few others to stimulate your interest and
inspiration.

section_title: ICT BULLISH PATTERN #1: OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY (OTE)
content:
Condition:
- Higher time frame has to be bullish.
Stage/Setup:
- Price bounces off a higher time frame discount array.
- An impulse swing creates a Fair Value Gap near the swing low.
- A short-term low forms in market structure and fails to rally higher after equal highs or a higher high is
formed.
- Price will eventually drop down into the Fair Value Gap and under the short-term low formed after the
impulse price swing.

section_title: DETAILED EXPLANATION OF BULLISH OTE PATTERN
content:
This is my fair value play or Optimal Trade Entry (OTE). It wasn't explained in this detail on the forums in
2010; people were simply looking for 62 to 79 retracement levels for OTE entries by pulling a Fibonacci
from the low up to the impulse swing.
When price attempts to rally higher and then drops down, the OTE levels (62, 70.5, or 79 retracement)
overlap with:
- A Fair Value Gap.
- A short-term low where sell stops reside.
- A bullish order block.
This gives you four confluences. If you have this with higher time frame bullishness, it's a 'lead pipe cinch
doozy.' If it goes up, it will go up strongly. While not a guarantee, my study shows this criteria works more
than any other. Often, it will rally, retrace, and there might not be a short-term low in between; if not, it's not
as high probability. But when I see this specific setup, this is the one I want to trade for fair value buys or
OTE.

section_title: BULLISH OTE CONFLUENCES AND INTERNAL RANGE LIQUIDITY
content:
This is an internal range liquidity, range expansion trade. It creates a range from the low at the order block
up to the impulse swing and then starts to retrace. Going long inside that retracing range is trading internal
range liquidity, with the expectation that price will expand outside of it. I'll be targeting external range
liquidity.

section_title: ICT BULLISH PATTERN #2: TURTLE SOUP (EXTERNAL RANGE LIQUIDITY)
content:
Condition:
- Higher time frame is bullish.
Stage/Setup:
- Price will bounce prior to a predetermined or anticipated higher time frame discount array.
- Price drops lower into the anticipated higher time frame discount array at a later time and raids sell stops
in the form of a liquidity pool.
- Sell stops are triggered. Smart money uses offset accumulation to pair long entries with the sell stop raid,
creating a discount entry.
This is the ICT version of 'Turtle Soup.'

section_title: DETAILED EXPLANATION OF BULLISH TURTLE SOUP
content:
In Linda Raschke's 'Street Smarts' book, the inspiration for this pattern, it's simply presented as 'price goes
below the low, buy it there.' In my opinion, that's myopic; there must be more understanding. This is how I
view it:
If I anticipate price coming down to a discount array (what others might call a support level), and price
stops just short of it or above it, hovering or moving up slightly, it creates a false bottom or fake low. I won't
buy that. I wait to see if they will run down and 'kill' those early bulls. When that short-term low is created
ahead of my desired buying level, everyone who bought there will have their stop loss (sell stop) below it. I
know that if price is going to reach into a deeper discount, I'll wait.
Patience pays here. I'm patient in trading because I know what I'm looking for. I want to see a low form
ahead of an anticipated discount level. For example, if I expect a Fair Value Gap to be filled, and price
comes down but doesn't quite get there, then starts to rally, I'll wait to see if that's a 'sucker play' before one
more push lower. When they do that, I know those sell stops will be triggered, hitting the marketplace as
sell-side liquidity. Smart money will accumulate and offset those stops. I buy down there at the discounted
rate because I now have a stronger conviction price should rally, having taken out stops and hit my logical
discount array. This is when 'Turtle Soup' works.

section_title: ICT BULLISH PATTERN #3: THE BULLISH BREAKER
content:
This is essentially the same pattern as Bullish Pattern 2, but used in the event that I missed the Turtle Soup
entry. If I couldn't get that entry, I wait for price to rally through the short-term high. That short-term high
now becomes a bullish breaker.
When price trades back down to the breaker, I use that as my entry. I know that after the stop run at the
lower low, price should not come back down there. There usually won't be a retracement down into an
order block where the initial buy level was because it's already done its work. Price will find support around
that bullish breaker, so I look at that level as my entry point.

section_title: BULLISH BREAKER AS CONTINGENCY OR PYRAMIDING ENTRY
content:
Within the impulse leg from the 'buy level' (after the stop run) up to the high, as price trades from the bullish
breaker, there will be a portion of price action on a lower time frame that represents an internal range
liquidity or Optimal Trade Entry. You won't see it directly on the higher time frame, but on a lower time
frame, it will be an OTE, trading into a lower time frame bullish order block or a Fair Value Gap, and then
price will expand, overlapping with that bullish breaker.
This pattern is universal across all time frames (monthly to one-minute). If I see this on a higher time frame,
I can reduce it down to look for its fractal on a smaller time frame. I look for this either as a new position
entry (if I missed the first one) or as an opportunity to pyramid. If I went long on the turtle soup, I can add to
that position at the bullish breaker. For example, if I bought 20 lots on the turtle soup, I might add 10 more
at the bullish breaker, confident it won't go back down into the internal range liquidity because it's already
run the stops and will move quickly away from that area to price for premium.

section_title: SUMMARY OF BULLISH PATTERNS AND TRADING DISCIPLINE
content:
I've covered essentially two patterns:
- External range liquidity (Turtle Soup).
- Internal range liquidity (Optimal Trade Entry).
I've also shown how, if I miss the Turtle Soup, I can use the bullish breaker as a contingency. So, I have a
contingency plan for both patterns. If I don't see price doing these three things, I don't do anything. Period.
If it doesn't look this clear to me in price, I do not trade.

section_title: BLENDING CONCEPTS FOR TARGETS AND THE LIMITS OF AUTOMATION
content:
This is the setup, not targeting. Targeting is covered earlier with the four-hour to Average Daily Range
projections. I cannot give a recipe that covers every scenario, such as 'Average Daily Range plus three
standard deviations of Central Bank Dealer's Range and five of Asian Range and 14 of Flout.' You have to
blend these things and keep working; that's why it's hard. It's not simply 'plug and play.'
Many think they can plug in my concepts and automate them, but there are too many variables. You have to
think about what you're looking for and how they overlap. You'll never automate the whole process. I've
talked to educated quants and algorithmic traders who tried, but it doesn't work that way. You might
automate a concept here or there, but not the entire process.

Because I have these three specific criteria, but only two patterns, I have:
- A buy program.
- A sell program.
- A 'if I get it wrong' program (a contingency plan if I miss an opportunity on one pattern).
This applies to both buy-side and sell-side patterns.

section_title: ICT BEARISH PATTERN #1: OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY (OTE)
content:
Condition:
- Higher time frame is bearish.
Stage/Setup:
- Price bounces off a higher time frame premium array.
- An impulse swing creates a Fair Value Gap near the swing high.
- A short-term high forms in the market structure and fails to drop lower after equal lows or a lower low is
formed.
- Price rallies up into the Fair Value Gap and above a short-term high forming after the impulse swing forms.

section_title: DETAILED EXPLANATION OF BEARISH OTE PATTERN
content:
Buy stops are triggered. Smart money uses offset distribution to pair short entries with the buy stop raid,
creating a premium entry. This is another fair value trade or Optimal Trade Entry. If you see a bullish order
block and a Fair Value Gap as price trades away, there will ideally be a short-term high in the time frame
you're looking for.
Price will try to go lower or fail to go lower, then rally back above that short-term high, closing the Fair Value
Gap, and trading back up to the bullish order block. That sell level is where the Optimal Trade Entry would
be, after the buy stops are run out on a previous short-term high.
If you use the high of the order block (or whatever the highest high is at that swing high) down to the lower
swing that failed to go much lower before hitting the Fair Value Gap, that's your impulse swing. The
retracement back up to the sell-side level or entry is the bearish order block. This pattern is just the reverse
of ICT Bullish Pattern 1; I'm selling short at a bearish order block.

section_title: ICT BEARISH PATTERN #2: TURTLE SOUP (EXTERNAL RANGE LIQUIDITY)
content:
Condition:
- Higher time frame is bearish.
Stage/Setup:
- Price is going to bounce prior to a higher time frame premium array (meaning you expect price to come
up higher, but it falls short).
- Price rallies higher into the anticipated higher time frame premium array and raids the buy stop liquidity
pool.
- Buy stops are triggered. Smart money uses offset distribution to pair short entries with the buy stop raid,
creating a premium entry.
This is the ICT classic 'Turtle Soup' entry.

section_title: DETAILED EXPLANATION OF BEARISH TURTLE SOUP
content:
This is the opposite of the bullish pattern 2, essentially a 'Turtle Soup' sell. All the things I look for make it a
'Turtle Soup.' Everyone used to ask, 'How do you know it's going to stop when it goes above the old high?'
Because you have to know the PD Array Matrix for that time frame. Where are the PD Arrays? Obviously,
we're in a bearish market environment when looking for this.
Scenario: Let's assume this is London. Price has been going lower; it's Wednesday. We had a high, then
started to drop, then rallied back up. We run through that high at London Open there's your Turtle Soup
entry. It runs out buy stops. Price didn't trade up to a premium array, not a deep enough retracement for
you. Maybe you see this on a four-hour chart and anticipate that expansion down the whole four-hour,
based on what you see, but it occurs during London or New York.
Say London is consolidation, and a news event comes out at 8:30 AM New York Open. Price runs up on
that news, hits that premium array, and then you can sell short, getting in sync with a nice day trade. Many
times, if New York is reversing, it can be a longer-term move.

section_title: ICT BEARISH PATTERN #3: THE BEARISH BREAKER
content:
This is based on the same idea of looking for the 'Turtle Soup,' but in the event that I was not able to get
positioned on that run above the buy stops. That run above the short-term high, tripping out buy stops,
reaching the premium array that price was likely to hit but didn't on its first attempt, then finally trades up to
it that's where I want to sell. But if I couldn't get in and price trades down through the breaker, once the
breaker is traded back up to, I can look to sell short there.
My confidence level is very strong because the breaker has done its job. It ran the buy stops, and price will
not want to give them an opportunity to go back up to that level. Don't expect an Optimal Trade Entry near
where it says the sell level (the higher premium array) and the low prior to that retrace back to the breaker.
That range down won't be a range that needs to be retraced back to. Many folks get confused, thinking
they'll get that range closed and a retracement; price usually won't go that deep. It's the breaker that stops
it, which is why I sell there.

section_title: BEARISH BREAKER AS CONTINGENCY OR PYRAMIDING ENTRY
content:
If I was fortunate enough to get short at the higher premium array, I can look at that bearish breaker as an
opportunity to pyramid. For example, if I went short 10 contracts at the higher premium array, I can now sell
short five more at the bearish breaker to pyramid my position.
So, I've covered all three bullish and all three bearish patterns, but again, it's primarily just two patterns:
- Internal range liquidity (Optimal Trade Entry, trading back to Fair Values).
- External range liquidity (Turtle Soup, running out stops and fading that move).
If I missed the Turtle Soup, I wait for it to go to a bearish breaker. That's it. It's the same two patterns, but
applied in a very specific, detailed way. If I don't see price doing these three things, I don't do anything. If it
doesn't look this clear in price, I do not trade.

section_title: PD ARRAY MATRIX AND CONSISTENCY IN ICT CONCEPTS
content:
Without understanding the PD Array Matrix and how we work from a higher time frame down to a lower
time frame with those same ideas, using institutional order flow and market structure, you will never be
consistent with my concepts. This is why folks who went through the free tutorials got frustrated; it lacked
clear, definitive rule-based ideas, which I did intentionally. Those tutorials were to see if anyone could
duplicate what I did; nobody has, even in the mentorship.

What we've covered is a fresh view on technical analysis. While many things might look similar to external
observers (supply and demand, Wyckoff), they are not the same. The problem with traditional support and
resistance is defining 'where is support?' If it was easy, you'd just draw a line. But it's not that easy; which
support level matters across different time frames? Using a higher time frame helps, but you still deal with
price falling short or going beyond a level. How much deviation do you allow, and what do you do with that
information?
We refine price from an institutional standpoint, knowing that our demo or live account will have slightly
skewed pricing. For intraday, we might see a range of 10 to 15 pips off interbank pricing. This adds risk to
day trading because you're already factoring in a range or spread you might not be aware of, as interbank
pricing isn't what our broker quotes. Brokers add another premium on top of that, creating a 'spread on top
of the spread.'

section_title: UNDERSTANDING INTERBANK PRICING AND SPREAD EFFECTS
content:
This makes it difficult; brokers can work that spread in their favor, opening it up to take your stop when you
get near a low or an old high (where everyone else's stops are). They won't let that spread help you when
it's beneficial for you. If you've traded with live funds, you'll know what I mean. It's amazing how you don't
see this problem in demo, but it becomes apparent with a live account.

section_title: THE SIGNIFICANCE OF BREAKERS AND LEARNING FROM LOSSES
content:
With the bearish pattern 3, we want to use that bearish breaker as either a pyramiding entry or a
secondary entry, or as our best opportunity to trade in the current market structure. Don't expect that the
premium array sell level and the low prior to that retrace back to the breaker will create an optimal trade
entry because doing so completely ignores the bearish breaker staring you in the face.
When I go through charts, the first thing I look for on all time frames is where the breakers are.
Understanding that gives me immediate context to work within. While I can still make mistakes (as you've
seen me do with breakers in live sessions), being wrong provides immediate feedback, allowing me to get
back in sync with the marketplace. Losing gives you premium insight. Don't fight it; you will lose in demo
and with live funds. Take the information it gives you as valuable insight.

- INTRODUCTION
Now, we'll go over the gold market to recap something that saves me from going into case studies and
provides an application of what you've been taught. I outlined this move and what to expect. During the live
session, 80 percent of you immediately targeted where the setup was going to be once I prompted you.
That's encouraging as a teacher and mentor you're able to understand the information, and see it without
me pointing to it. Once I did point to it, everyone confirmed it. Let's go into the charts.

- 4-HOUR BULLISH BIAS
On the gold four-hour chart, during last week's live sessions (specifically August 23rd, around the 1 hour
and 30 minute mark of part two of the recording), I gave a clear condition-stage-execution format. We went
through the monthly and daily charts, and the outline was for gold to be bullish.
We had to see price trade lower to pick up a discount array. We identified two lows and needed to wait for a
trade lower because we can't go long while price is already rising on a four-hour chart when we're looking
for a discount.

- HOURLY CHART & FAIR VALUE GAP
Dropping down to an hourly chart, we have a low and a Fair Value Gap where only buy-side was offered
(no sell-side delivery). This range, between 1280 and 1278 (two handles), was exclusively buy-side offered.

- DISCOUNT ARRAYS & STOP PLACEMENT
Prior to this gap, the next downside discount array is a swing high (with a lower high to its left and right).
Below that, there's another swing high. So, we have a discount array here, then another, then an order
block below the Fair Value Gap. We're using the PD Array Matrix. Our stop must be below this level,
specifically below 1276. The entire candle's low is a bit below that, so we refine our stop level to 12.75 and
below. We'd be a buyer if it fills the gap, with a protective stop below 12.75, perhaps at 12.74.

- OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY (OTE) REFINEMENT
This entry would be a run on sell stops and trading on external range liquidity for this low, but internal range
liquidity for the low up to this high. We have to be a buyer at a discount and pair that with a premium array.
We need to see it trade down below this low for an external range liquidity run, which we could couple with
an internal range liquidity run here. Drawing a Fibonacci from the low up to the high, the Optimal Trade
Entry is right down here, at the 70.5 to 79 retracement levels. The Fair Value Gap is also here. The sweet
spot of that is the actual entry, with an overlap of 70.5, allowing for spread past the gap, or 78, which closes
the gap. We might allow spreading to the 70.5 level or Sweet Spot OTE. So, we're looking to go long at
12.78, with a stop at 12.74.

- PRICE ACTION & MISSED ENTRY SCENARIO
Here's our low. We wanted to see price go down into the Fair Value Gap, but it didn't; it stopped short. Price
then rallied. Are we buying here? No. Price goes up closer to a Fair Value Gap, then sells off, trading back
up into a short-term high. All this time, our plan is to buy down at the Fair Value Gap around 12.78, with a
stop at 12.74. Price hits 12.90. The bearish order block runs a short-term high, spiking up first, runs these
highs, then trades down into the gap.

- SUCCESSFUL OTE ENTRY
So we go long at 12.78. Our protective stop at 12.74 was never hit. We picked up a discount array, buying
sell stops below these lows. Price immediately rallies back up, and that's where we currently are.

- USING THE BREAKER AS A CONTINGENCY
Now, if we missed this opportunity (as most likely some did), we can use the breaker to get long. Where is
the breaker? We have this high prior to this low. There's an area here, but it's not as defined. This one here
is lower than that one, and we've traded above this high. So, I would err on the side of saying these candles
here are going to be the breaker. As long as we're buying inside this range, extended in time, this could be
a good area to be a buyer for gold. We could see it pick up and rally again. This example was discussed in
advance.

It took me minutes to explain, show, and outline this, despite all the concepts we've covered in these four
teachings seemingly being a lot. It doesn't take forever to go through. You watch me go through the charts,
and within minutes, I have an idea of what I want to do, where I think price is going to go, and how it should
react. This is one more example. I gave it to you with the Euro this week, and while we missed two runs,
the Euro eventually delivered on Friday.

The long and short of it is: you have all the components to make your own trading plan with what's been
taught to you. I've given you mine, showed you mine, and held back nothing. There's no secret, no hidden
information. I've given you everything I know about how I look at Forex, how I work in this demo account,
show you where it's going to go beforehand, and you see when it fails.
When it's right, it's very accurate. When it's wrong, it's wrong; there's nothing else to say. I told you upfront I
can't be perfect, and you'll never be perfect, so give yourself permission to be. You don't need to carry the
weight of knowing everything. You need to be right once in a while and be more right than you are wrong. If
you can do that, your career is brilliant ahead of you.

This does not complete our mentorship content yet. There are subtle nuances I'll discuss in the final week
of live sessions and recordings. I have a handful of topical studies to share:
- How I go through IBD to pick out stocks.
- The Greeks on options (as it ties into one teaching).
- Short synopsis top-down for bonds, S&P, and commodities (each as a respective topical study next week,
short and sweet).
It's been a lot of fun teaching this, very draining energetically, and I've dealt with some challenges. But I'm
100 percent confident that if you put the work into what I've shown you (how I outlined it, where to look for
information, when to look for it, and its use), everything you've aspired to do as a trader in technicals and
price, you have it now. The 'brick walls' have been reduced to 'speed bumps'; there's no mountain in front of
you.
You have every advantage I didn't. I've shown you everything that has worked for me in doing analysis and
picking market moves. There's no secret teaching in 2018 or the future, no book being written by me that
holds anything back. I've taught you everything I know and do in technicals, including other asset classes. I
wish there was more to dig deeper into, but you have reached the end of what makes ICT, ICT, aside from
topical studies and a few final things. It's been my pleasure to be your mentor. You still have much work
ahead to build your own model, and it's limitless. I cannot wait to hear back from each of you, telling me
what you did with it, how you formed your plan, and how you're trading with it. Until the future, good luck
and good trading.
